stories.headline: Update: Juvenile arrested in fatal shooting of 14-year-old at Montbello Recreation Center, victim identified
stories.summary:
A 14-year-old Denver boy, identified as Mikail Nasir Khalid Payne, was shot and killed inside the Montbello Recreation Center on Thursday evening. Denver police arrested a juvenile male on suspicion of first-degree murder and said investigators believe the two minors knew each other and that the incident was isolated.

Mikail's family said on a GoFundMe page that he was not involved in gangs or street activity and was shot at close range while defending his brother during an altercation. His family described him as bright, caring, and dedicated to helping his parents in their landscaping business.

Mayor Mike Johnston visited the recreation center the night of the shooting and offered condolences to the Montbello community. The center was closed Friday with all programs temporarily canceled, and a hand-written sign on the door indicated it would remain shut until further notice.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas said the department believes it was an isolated incident. State law prohibits release of records involving juvenile arrestees, and no further details about the suspect were disclosed.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis signs overhaul of Colorado competency law, providing $30 million for mental health beds and services
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed Senate Bill 149 on Thursday, overhauling Colorado's competency law to ensure that criminal defendants found incompetent to stand trial who pose a public safety threat are not automatically released into the community. The bill creates a pathway to connect such defendants with mental health services and allocates roughly $30 million to expand inpatient and outpatient capacity.

The legislation came after a 2024 law change required judges to dismiss charges against suspects found incompetent and unlikely to be restored, closing off judicial discretion that had previously existed. The new law reopens the door to retrials for defendants accused of serious crimes while still allowing misdemeanor charges to be dismissed.

Denver7 Investigates had spent more than a year exposing gaps in the existing law, highlighting cases where charges were dropped against violent defendants, some of which resulted in victims' deaths. One high-profile case involved Guillermo Ramirez, whose charges were dropped after he was found incompetent following a crash that killed a passenger in his car and later a crash victim's mother.

State Sen. Judy Amabile, D-Boulder, one of the bill's primary sponsors, said the law improves access to treatment while protecting constitutional rights. Victim advocate Joseph Bowman, who testified multiple times at the Capitol, called the bill signing a "solemn victory."

stories.headline: Trump administration releases $40 million in frozen funds for Colorado River Shoshone water rights purchase
stories.summary:
The U.S. Bureau of Reclamation has released $40 million in federal funding to the Colorado River District for the purchase of senior water rights attached to Xcel Energy's aging Shoshone Power Plant in Glenwood Canyon, bringing total secured funds to $97 million of the $99 million needed. The money, originally awarded under the Inflation Reduction Act in the final days of the Biden administration, had been frozen after President Trump took office.

The Colorado River District, a taxpayer-funded agency based in Glenwood Springs, has been working to acquire the rights to ensure that billions of gallons of water continue flowing west through Glenwood Canyon to downstream communities, farms and wildlife habitats even in dry years. District leaders feared that a Front Range utility could otherwise purchase the rights and redirect the flows.

Members of Colorado's bipartisan federal delegation, including Republican Rep. Jeff Hurd and Democrats Rep. Joe Neguse, Sen. Michael Bennet and Sen. John Hickenlooper, advocated for the release of the funds. District General Manager Andy Mueller called it a "once-in-a-generation" investment in securing Western Slope water supplies.

The deal, originally inked in 2023, still requires approval from Colorado's public utility regulators for Xcel Energy to complete the sale, and the river district must complete the state's water court process. The funding release brings the total to $97 million, combining the federal award with $20 million from the Colorado Water Conservation Board and $37 million raised from Western Slope governments and irrigators.

stories.headline: State agency converts $5 million Burnham Yard loan to grant to support Broncos stadium infrastructure
stories.summary:
Colorado's Economic Development Commission approved a request Thursday to convert $5 million of a $7.5 million loan owed to the Colorado Office of Economic Development and International Trade into a grant, freeing funds to support infrastructure work tied to the proposed Denver Broncos stadium at Burnham Yard. The remaining $2.5 million will be repaid into the state's Strategic Fund.

CDOT purchased the 58-acre Burnham Yard from Union Pacific in 2021 for $50 million using the OEDIT loan, a $7.5 million down payment and a $35 million commercial bank loan, with plans to widen and straighten Interstate 25 between Colfax and Santa Fe. Those plans were later abandoned after CDOT found the land more contaminated than expected and determined the highway improvements would cost billions more than available.

CDOT subsequently agreed to sell the parcel to the Broncos for $45.8 million — less than its purchase price — as part of a proposed $4 billion stadium and entertainment district. The converted grant money will fund a relocation of the main rail line, improved road access from I-25, and the elimination of three at-grade rail crossings.

State officials argued the "modest investment" in public infrastructure would leverage billions in private investment. Colorado is currently facing a $1.5 billion budget shortfall and lost jobs last year, pressures that added urgency to the commission's decision to approve the conversion.

stories.headline: Three Colorado quantum computing companies to receive $100 million each in federal equity-stake investment
stories.summary:
Atom Computing in Boulder, Infleqtion in Louisville and Quantinuum in Broomfield will each receive $100 million in federal funding as part of a $2 billion investment by the U.S. Department of Commerce in nine quantum computing companies nationwide. In exchange, the federal government will receive a minority, noncontrolling equity stake in each company under the CHIPS and Science Act.

The awards represent a significant influx of capital for Colorado's growing quantum ecosystem, which in 2023 was designated a national Tech Hub for quantum computing. The state subsequently received a $40.5 million federal grant to support commercial development, job training and startup activity in the sector.

Infleqtion, which went public in February after raising $550 million in its IPO, said it will issue common stock valued at $100 million at a 15% discount to the Commerce Department. Atom Computing, which employs about 80 people in Colorado and has raised more than $200 million to date, said the funding will accelerate development of utility-scale quantum systems.

Broomfield-based Quantinuum, which has been eyeing a public offering, also signed a letter of intent. Colorado officials said the federal investment validates the state's position as a global center for quantum innovation and is expected to attract additional private capital to the sector.

stories.headline: Avalanche fall to Vegas 3-1 in Game 2, trail Western Conference Final 2-0
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche dropped Game 2 of the Western Conference Final to the Vegas Golden Knights 3-1 on Friday night, falling into a 2-0 series hole. Jack Eichel and Ivan Barbashev scored in a span of 2:07 in the third period to seal the victory for Vegas.

The loss compounds the challenge facing Colorado, which is also dealing with the absence of defenseman Cale Makar, who was sidelined heading into the series. Makar is one of the team's most important players and his status for upcoming games remains uncertain.

The Avalanche now face a steep climb, as no team in NHL history has come back from a 3-0 series deficit. Games 3 and 4 will shift to Ball Arena in Denver, giving Colorado the home-ice advantage it will need to keep its season alive.

stories.headline: Denver man sues waste disposal company after being crushed in garbage truck compactor while sleeping in dumpster
stories.summary:
A Denver man, Steven Egloff, filed a negligence lawsuit Monday in Denver District Court against Waste Connections of Colorado after he was seriously injured in April 2025 when a garbage truck emptied a RiNo neighborhood dumpster in which he was sleeping. The truck's driver started the compactor before hearing Egloff's screams at a subsequent stop, prompting a 911 call.

Egloff sustained a large scalp laceration with degloving injury, multiple broken ribs, a broken right upper arm in at least two places and a fractured vertebra, according to the lawsuit. He required extensive hospitalization and is now recovering in Louisiana with his sister.

The lawsuit accuses the company of failing to require dumpster checks before collection, failing to inspect the back of the truck before operating the compactor, and failing to properly train staff to prevent such incidents. The suit argues that people experiencing homelessness seeking shelter in dumpsters is a known and foreseeable hazard in the waste collection industry.

Egloff's attorney said his client climbed into the dumpster because it was cold and he lacked a jacket. The incident is one of at least three similar dumpster-related incidents in Colorado in the past year, including a Loveland man who went missing after entering a dumpster and a Pueblo man who died after being found in the back of a garbage truck.

stories.headline: Broomfield space startup Katalyst ships spacecraft to NASA for mission to rescue aging observatory from orbital decay
stories.summary:
Katalyst Space Technologies, a startup with its primary manufacturing operations in Broomfield, is shipping a robotic spacecraft called Link to NASA ahead of a June launch to rescue the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory. Built in just eight months from concept, Link is designed to approach the tumbling observatory, grab hold of it and boost it into a higher orbit before it burns up in Earth's atmosphere later in 2026.

The Swift Observatory, launched in 2004 to study gamma ray bursts and other high-energy cosmic phenomena, has been decaying faster than expected due to increased solar activity. Replacing the observatory, which originally cost $500 million to build, would be far more expensive than attempting the rescue, which NASA has called a "fast, high-risk, high-reward mission."

Katalyst, an Arizona-based aerospace company that established most of its infrastructure in the Denver metro area after acquiring Atomos Space last year, beat a team that included Denver's Astroscale U.S. to win the $30 million NASA contract. The mission would be the first of its kind, as the Swift Observatory was built before satellites were designed to accommodate life-extension servicing.

The observatory has a notable Colorado connection: it was conceived in the basement of the Stanley Hotel in Estes Park. Success would mark a milestone for Colorado's commercial space industry and for the broader satellite servicing market, which is growing as low Earth orbit becomes increasingly crowded.

stories.headline: Democratic AG candidate Hetal Doshi launches six-figure TV and digital ad campaign in Colorado primary
stories.summary:
Democratic attorney general candidate Hetal Doshi launched her first television and digital ad statewide Friday, kicking off a six-figure buy just over two weeks before mail ballots begin going out to Colorado primary voters on June 8. The 30-second spot, set to a 1968 Camaro Super Sport backdrop, introduces the candidate and her name pronunciation with the tagline "Hetal to the metal."

Doshi, a first-generation Indian American and former lead antitrust litigator at the U.S. Department of Justice, is one of four Democrats vying for the seat held by term-limited Attorney General Phil Weiser, who is seeking the Democratic gubernatorial nomination. Her ad highlights her work on major prosecutions involving Google, Ticketmaster and a former Department of Veterans Affairs employee.

Recent polling released by a rival campaign shows Doshi faces significant ground to make up. A survey conducted last month for Secretary of State Jena Griswold's campaign found Griswold with 46% support among likely Democratic primary voters, followed by workers' rights attorney David Seligman and Boulder DA Michael Dougherty at 7% each, with Doshi at 5% and 35% undecided.

Two Republicans — 4th Judicial District Attorney Michael Allen and Colorado Springs lawyer David Willson — are competing in the GOP primary for the nomination.

stories.headline: Part of prefabricated home falls from semi-truck on Colorado 62 near Dallas Divide, closing highway
stories.summary:
Half of a prefabricated home fell off a semitrailer on Colorado Highway 62 approximately four miles from the Dallas Divide in San Miguel County early Friday morning, fully blocking both lanes of traffic. The San Miguel County Sheriff's Office reported the incident occurred just before 7 a.m., with no injuries reported.

The fallen structure, covered in a white tarp, straddled both lanes and required a crane and bulldozer to help clear debris from the roadway. Images showed that one wall of the building had separated, scattering furniture including dressers across the highway.

The Colorado State Patrol responded and did not immediately identify a cause for the accident or provide an estimated timeline for reopening the route. Highway 62 connects Ridgway to Telluride and is a primary corridor for the region.

stories.headline: Warmer, drier weather expected across Denver metro for Memorial Day weekend before storms return next week
stories.summary:
After scattered showers lingering into Friday evening, Denver and the Front Range are expected to see a warmer and mostly drier stretch through the Memorial Day weekend. Saturday will bring a mix of sun and clouds with only isolated afternoon mountain showers possible, and Sunday will see highs climbing into the 80s across the plains.

Monday brings increased moisture and a higher chance of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms as weather systems shift across the western United States. Temperatures on Monday are expected to remain mild and slightly above seasonal averages.

Beginning Tuesday and continuing through the following week, forecasters expect a more active pattern with scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms developing most days. Some storms during that period could become stronger, though temperatures are expected to remain comfortably warm.

stories.headline: Memorial Day weekend events include Colorado Remembers ceremony, Boulder Creek Festival and Denver night markets
stories.summary:
The Denver metro area and surrounding region are hosting a range of Memorial Day weekend events, beginning Friday with the AAPI Culture Fest and Night Market at Civic Center Park and an Asian American, Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander celebration at Denver International Airport's Park on the Plaza. Both events are free and open to the public.

On Saturday morning, the Colorado Freedom Memorial in Aurora will host the 13th annual Colorado Remembers ceremony, honoring Colorado's fallen veterans and those still serving. This year's ceremony also marks the 250th anniversary of American independence. The Boulder Creek Festival runs through Monday along Boulder Creek between 9th and 14th streets, featuring live music, artisan vendors and a Saturday screening of Napoleon Dynamite in partnership with the Sundance Film Festival.

Additional free offerings include the Children's Museum of Denver's Joy Park Free Night on Friday evening and the Music in the Gardens lunchtime concert series at the 17th Street Community Gardens. Grand-opening ceremonies are also planned in Gunnison and Grand Lake for those making a holiday road trip.

weather.notable: Nearly 7 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Autocorrect changed 'I'm on my way' to 'I'm in my way.' Which is honestly more accurate.

topStory.headline: Federal Judge Dismisses Human Smuggling Case Against Kilmar Abrego García, Finds Prosecution Vindictive
topStory.summary:
A federal judge in Nashville on Friday dismissed criminal human smuggling charges against Kilmar Abrego García, a Salvadoran migrant at the center of a prolonged legal battle with the Trump administration over his deportation. U.S. District Judge Waverly D. Crenshaw Jr. ruled in a 32-page opinion that the Justice Department's prosecution amounted to vindictive retaliation, finding that charges would not have been filed had Abrego García not successfully sued the government over his removal to El Salvador. Crenshaw also dismissed the conditions of Abrego García's pre-trial release as part of his ruling.

Crenshaw found sufficient evidence of "presumptive vindictiveness," pointing specifically to the timing of the indictment and the conduct of senior Justice Department officials. The judge wrote that the government closed its investigation stemming from a November 2022 Tennessee traffic stop after Abrego García was deported, only to reopen it after he prevailed in civil court. Crenshaw identified then-Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche, now serving as acting attorney general, as having initiated the investigation to justify the administration's decision to deport Abrego García to El Salvador.

Abrego García was deported to El Salvador in March 2025 despite an existing immigration court order barring his removal there on the grounds he could face persecution. The Trump administration brought him back to the United States in June 2025 after the Supreme Court ordered officials to facilitate his return, but federal prosecutors had by then secured an indictment accusing him of accepting money to transport undocumented individuals within the country. The charges arose from a 2022 traffic stop in which Abrego García was pulled over for speeding and found to have nine passengers in his vehicle.

The Justice Department said it would appeal the ruling, calling the judge's order "wrong and dangerous" and characterizing Crenshaw as "another activist judge" who placed politics above public safety. Abrego García's defense attorneys said in a statement that their client "is a victim of a politicized, vindictive White House and its lawyers at what used to be an independent Justice Department." The ruling drew attention to a press conference last year at which then-Attorney General Pam Bondi touted the charges, declaring, "This is what American justice looks like."

Despite the dismissal, Abrego García's legal situation remains unresolved. Administration officials have threatened to deport him to several African countries, most recently Liberia, after being barred from returning him to El Salvador. With pre-trial release conditions now lifted, Abrego García may be free to travel to Costa Rica, which has publicly offered him asylum — an option he has expressed willingness to pursue but which the administration has so far refused to accept.
